title: Example Ordering Effects
concept: Recency bias and order sensitivity in few-shot prompts; why shuffling examples changes outputs.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we learned that example selection matters. Why should we select diverse and representative examples instead of random ones?

options.text: It makes the prompt shorter and cheaper.
It helps the LLM generalize better and avoid repeating the same bias.
It guarantees the model will never hallucinate again.

exercises.explain: The prior lesson taught that selecting diverse, representative, and correctly-labeled examples is crucial for few-shot performance, rather than just picking random or repetitive ones.

cards.title: Order Matters!
cards.body: You might think that providing the exact same three examples in a prompt will always yield the same result. Surprisingly, just changing their order can completely flip the LLM's final answer. Let's find out why!

cards.title: What is Ordering Sensitivity?
cards.body: Example Ordering Effects refer to how changing the order of your few-shot examples alters the LLM's response. This happens because models have 'recency bias', meaning they pay extra attention to the examples closest to the end of the prompt.

cards.title: The Flashcard Effect
cards.body: Imagine you are studying flashcards right before an exam. The very last flashcard you look at is freshest in your mind, so you are most likely to use its exact words on the test. LLMs do this too, often over-weighting the very last example they see before the prompt ends.

cards.title: Shuffling the Output
cards.body: If you put a positive sentiment example last in your prompt, the LLM might classify a neutral text as positive. If you swap the order and put a negative example last, the model might label that same neutral text as negative simply because of its position.

cards.title: The Equal Weight Myth
cards.body: Many developers believe that LLMs read and weigh all few-shot examples completely equally. In reality, models are highly sensitive to sequence, often treating the last example as a stronger blueprint than the first one.

cards.title: Attention Focus
cards.body: How the LLM's attention shifts depending on where examples are placed in the prompt.

left.text: Often gets less attention or gets diluted as the prompt grows longer.

right.text: Gets the most attention due to its proximity to the final question.

exercises.prompt: Due to recency bias, which example in a few-shot prompt does an LLM tend to over-weight?

options.text: The first example in the prompt
The last example right before the final question
The example with the longest text
The example containing the most punctuation

exercises.explain: Because of recency bias, the LLM is highly likely to mimic the style, formatting, or sentiment of the very last example it reads before the final question.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Shuffling the order of your few-shot examples will never change the LLM's final classification.
exercises.explain: Even with the exact same examples, changing their order shifts the attention mechanism of the model, which can lead to different outputs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe why LLMs pay more attention to the examples closest to the end of the prompt:
exercises.sentence: LLMs often suffer from recency bias because they over-focus on the final examples in the prompt.
exercises.explain: Recency bias causes the model to place disproportionate weight on the examples it processed most recently (the ones at the end of the prompt).

exercises.prompt: Identify the statement that sounds like an AI explaining why example order alters LLM behavior:

options.text: 'I changed the layout of my database and now my queries are running slightly slower.'
'We need to add more diverse training data to prevent our neural network from overfitting.'
'When evaluating few-shot prompts, changing the sequence of input-output pairs shifts the attention weights, leading the LLM to over-index on the final exemplar.'

exercises.explain: Option C shows a typical LLM hallucination/explanation that correctly describes the behavior of order sensitivity and recency bias in context window attention. Options A and B are generic developer statements.

exercises.prompt: Why is it a best practice to shuffle the order of your few-shot examples during testing and evaluation?

options.text: It helps detect recency bias and ensures the prompt's performance is stable regardless of example order.
It automatically increases the model's processing speed by 50%.
It forces the model to ignore the final user query entirely.
It ensures that only the worst-performing examples are processed first.

exercises.explain: LLMs suffer from recency bias (over-focusing on the last example) and general order sensitivity. Shuffling the order of your examples allows you to test if your prompt is robust, or if it only works when one specific example is placed at the end.
